papergraph.title: Graph-guided autonomous agents for scientific discovery
papergraph.citation: Li Wang et al. (2026) Graph-guided autonomous agents for scientific discovery doi:10.1234/example.2026.001
papergraph.abstract: We present a multi-agent research system that combines literature graphs, hypothesis generation, and mechanism validation. The method improves coverage of interdisciplinary evidence and reduces duplicated citation handling in long-running research projects.
papergraph.conclusion: Graph-guided agents can improve scientific workflow reliability when citation identity, evidence credibility, and limitation tracking are handled explicitly.
strengths: Abstract: We present a multi-agent research system that combines literature graphs, hypothesis generation, and mechanism validation. The method improves coverage of interdisciplinary evidence and reduces duplicated citation handling in long-running research projects.
Conclusion: Graph-guided agents can improve scientific workflow reliability when citation identity, evidence credibility, and limitation tracking are handled explicitly.
Strengths: Strong integration of evidence provenance. Clear task decomposition. Useful for project-scale reasoning.
improvements: Conclusion: Graph-guided agents can improve scientific workflow reliability when citation identity, evidence credibility, and limitation tracking are handled explicitly.
Limitations: The current prototype relies on heuristic parsing and lacks live publisher API integration.
Future work: Add robust PDF extraction and external database connectors.
papergraph.method: The method improves coverage of interdisciplinary evidence and reduces duplicated citation handling in long-running research projects.
papergraph.scenario: We evaluate the system on materials science and biomedical discovery tasks.
papergraph.contribution: Abstract: We present a multi-agent research system that combines literature graphs, hypothesis generation, and mechanism validation. The method improves coverage of interdisciplinary evidence and reduces duplicated citation handling in long-running research projects.
papergraph.limitation: Conclusion: Graph-guided agents can improve scientific workflow reliability when citation identity, evidence credibility, and limitation tracking are handled explicitly.
